revision.text: #redirect[[en:Tomo_no_miyatsuko]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

== '''伴造 とものみやつこ''' ==
伴造とは、連(むらじ)とも重なり、また連の下で朝廷の各部司を分掌した豪族である。
秦 (はた)、東漢 (やまとのあや)、西漢 (かわちのあや) など代表的な帰化氏族である。 他に、弓削 (ゆげ)、矢集 (やずめ)、服部 (はとり)、犬養 (いぬかい)、舂米 (つきしね)、倭文 (しとり) などの氏がある。
彼らは、連、造 (みやつこ)、直 (あたい)、公 (きみ)などの姓 (かばね) を称した。
==関連項目==
*[[氏姓制度]] *[[古代]] *[[律令制]]

== '''伴造 とものみやつこ''' ==
伴造とは、連(むらじ)とも重なり、また連の下で朝廷の各部司を分掌した豪族である。連は、[[大伴氏|大伴]]、[[物部氏|物部]]、[[中臣氏|中臣]]、[[忌部|忌部氏]] (いんべ)、[[土師氏|土師]] (はじ) のように、天皇に従属するであり、朝廷の成立に重要な役割をはたした豪族である。
伴造には、秦 (はた)、東漢 (やまとのあや)、西漢 (かわちのあや) など代表的な帰化氏族がある。 他に、弓削 (ゆげ)、矢集 (やずめ)、服部 (はとり)、犬養 (いぬかい)、舂米 (つきしね)、倭文 (しとり) などの氏がある。
彼らは、連、造 (みやつこ)、直 (あたい)、公 (きみ)などの姓 (かばね) を称した。
==関連項目==
*[[氏姓制度]] *[[古代]] *[[律令制]]

'''伴造'''（とものみやつこ）は、[[連]]（むらじ）とも重なり、また連の下で[[ヤマト王権]]の各部司を分掌した[[豪族]]である。
「伴」は友・供と同音でヤマト王権の長である[[治天下大王|大王]]に奉仕する意味があり、「造」は集団の長としての意味があった。伴造には連のような上位の[[カバネ|姓]]（かばね）を持つ者も含まれるが、狭義では造・[[首]]（おびと）などの中位以下の姓を持つ者を指す<ref>狩野『日本史大事典』</ref>。また、下級の姓の中には伴造から除外されて[[百八十部]]の身分に属する者もいた<ref>平野『国史大辞典』</ref>。
伴造とされた豪族はそれぞれの職掌を持ち、傘下の部民を率いてヤマト王権の中枢に人的あるいは物的に奉仕した。
伴造には、[[秦氏]]（はた）、[[東漢氏]]（やまとのあや）、[[西文氏]]（かわちのふみ）など代表的な[[渡来人|帰化氏族]]がある。他に、[[弓削氏|弓削]]（ゆげ）、[[矢集氏|矢集]]（やずめ）、[[服部氏|服部]]（はとり）、[[犬養部|犬養]]（いぬかい）、[[舂米]]（つきしね）、[[倭文]]（しとり）などの[[氏]]がある。彼らは、連、[[造]]（みやつこ）、[[直]]（あたい）、[[公]]（きみ）などの姓（かばね）を称した。
律令制において[[品部]]・[[雑戸]]などを率いて器具の製作などの実務を担当する下級官人である[[伴部]]にその名残が見られ、伴部を「とものみやつこ」と読む用法が存在したことが知られている。

revision.comment: 「帰化」は国籍を前提とした用語であり、実質上の国境警備施設である渟足柵や太宰府などが設置される以前に日本化した一族に「帰化」は不適当であるため最近の歴史学ではこの用語を用いないのでこれに従って修正した。

'''伴造'''（とものみやつこ）は、[[連]]（むらじ）とも重なり、また連の下で[[ヤマト王権]]の各部司を分掌した[[豪族]]である。
「伴」は友・供と同音でヤマト王権の長である[[大王 (ヤマト王権)|大王]]（おおきみ）に奉仕する意味があり、「造」は集団の長としての意味があった。伴造には連のような上位の[[カバネ|姓]]（かばね）を持つ者も含まれるが、狭義では造・[[首 (姓)|首]]（おびと）などの中位以下の姓を持つ者を指す<ref>狩野『日本史大事典』</ref>。また、下級の姓の中には伴造から除外されて[[百八十部]]の身分に属する者もいた<ref>平野『国史大辞典』</ref>。
伴造とされた豪族はそれぞれの職掌を持ち、傘下の部民を率いてヤマト王権の中枢に人的あるいは物的に奉仕した。
伴造には、[[秦氏]]（はた）、[[東漢氏]]（やまとのあや）、[[西文氏]]（かわちのふみ）など代表的な[[渡来人]]がある。他に、[[弓削氏|弓削]]（ゆげ）、[[矢集氏|矢集]]（やずめ）、[[服部氏|服部]]（はとり）、[[犬養部|犬養]]（いぬかい）、[[舂米氏|舂米]]（つきしね）、[[倭文氏|倭文]]（しとり）などの[[氏#古代氏族としての「氏」|氏]]（うじ）がある。彼らは、連、[[造]]（みやつこ）、[[直 (姓)|直]]（あたい）、[[キミ (カバネ)|君・公]]（きみ）などの姓（かばね）を称した。
律令制において[[品部]]・[[雑戸]]などを率いて器具の製作などの実務を担当する下級官人である[[伴部]]にその名残が見られ、伴部を「とものみやつこ」と読む用法が存在したことが知られている。
== 脚注 == <references/> == 参考文献 == *平野邦雄「伴造」（『[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]] 10』（[[吉川弘文館]]、1989年） ISBN 978-4-642-00510-4） *狩野久「伴造」（『[[日本史大事典]] 5』（吉川弘文館、1993年） ISBN 978-4-642-00510-4） *森公章「伴造」（『[[日本歴史大事典]] 3』（[[小学館]]、2001年） ISBN 978-4-09-523003-0）
== 関連項目 == * [[氏姓制度]] * [[部民制]] * [[人制]] * [[国造]]

[[Category:kkk]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Tomo no miyatsuko]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:Tomo no miyatsuko]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:Kk]] [[ja:%E4%BC%B4%E9%80%A0]]

'''Tomonomiyatsuko''' (伴造) were aristocratic clans of the [[Yamato Kingship]] (ヤマト王権) who managed various governmental roles, overlapping with or working under the {{ill|Muraji|ja|連}} class.
The term "伴" (tomo) is phonetically similar to "友" (companion) or "供" (attendant) and signifies service to the {{ill|Ōkimi|ja|大王 (ヤマト王権)}} (Great King). Meanwhile, "造" (miyatsuko) denotes leadership over a group. Some Tomonomiyatsuko held high-ranking {{ill|Kabane|ja|カバネ}} titles like Muraji, but in a narrower sense, it referred to those with mid-to-lower ranks such as **Zō (造)** and **Obito (首, おびと)**.<ref>Kano, *Nihonshi Daijiten* (日本史大事典)</ref> Additionally, those from lower-ranking families were sometimes excluded from Tomonomiyatsuko status and categorized under the {{ill|Hyakuhachibu|ja|百八十部}} class.<ref>Hirano, *Kokushi Daijiten* (国史大辞典)</ref>
Each Tomonomiyatsuko clan had specific administrative duties and led **Be (部民)** groups, providing **human labor or material support** to the Yamato government.
Under the **Ritsuryō system**, remnants of the Tomonomiyatsuko system persisted in the **Banbu (伴部)** class, which oversaw craftsmanship and administrative work for the state. Records suggest that Banbu was still read as "Tomonomiyatsuko" in certain contexts.
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Hirano Kunio, "Tomonomiyatsuko," in *{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa era)|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}} 10* (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) ISBN 978-4-642-00510-4 * Kano Hisashi, "Tomonomiyatsuko," in *{{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典}} 5* (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1993) ISBN 978-4-642-00510-4 * Mori Kōshō, "Tomonomiyatsuko," in *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典}} 3* (Shogakukan, 2001) ISBN 978-4-09-523003-0

Under the **Ritsuryō system**, remnants of the Tomonomiyatsuko system persisted in the **Banbu (伴部)** class, which oversaw craftsmanship and administrative work for the state. Records suggest that Banbu was still read as "Tomonomiyatsuko" in certain contexts.

Meanwhile, "造" (miyatsuko) denotes leadership over a group. Some Tomonomiyatsuko held high-ranking {{ill|Kabane|ja|カバネ}} titles like Muraji, but in a narrower sense, it referred to those with mid-to-lower ranks such as **Zō (造)** and **Obito (首, おびと)**.<ref>Kano, *Nihonshi Daijiten* (日本史大事典)</ref> Additionally, those from lower-ranking families were sometimes excluded from Tomonomiyatsuko status and categorized under the {{ill|Hyakuhachibu|ja|百八十部}} class.<ref>Hirano, *Kokushi Daijiten* (国史大辞典)</ref>
Each Tomonomiyatsuko clan had specific administrative duties and led **Be (部民)** groups, providing **human labor or material support** to the Yamato government.
Under the **Ritsuryō system**, remnants of the Tomonomiyatsuko system persisted in the **Banbu (伴部)** class, which oversaw craftsmanship and administrative work for the state. Records suggest that Banbu was still read as "Tomonomiyatsuko" in certain contexts.

[[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Toraijin]] [[Category:Hata clan]] [[ja:伴造]]
[[Category:kkk]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomonomiyatsuko]][[Category:qq]] <!--Tomonomiyatsuko-->[[Category:Tomo no miyatsuko]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:goodbye $1]] [[Category:Tomo no miyatsuko]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:goodbye $1]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:人制]] [[Category:氏姓制度]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomonomiyatsuko]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomonomiyatsuko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomonomiyatsuko]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomonomiyatsuko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomonomiyatsuko]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomonomiyatsuko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomonomiyatsuko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomonomiyatsuko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomonomiyatsuko]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomonomiyatsuko]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomonomiyatsuko]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tomonomiyatsuko]][[ja:%E4%BC%B4%E9%80%A0]]

'''Tomonomiyatsuko''' (伴造) were aristocratic clans of the [[Yamato Kingship]] (ヤマト王権) who managed various governmental roles, overlapping with or working under the {{ill|Muraji|ja|連}} class.
The term "伴" (tomo) is phonetically similar to "友" (companion) or "供" (attendant) and signifies service to the {{ill|Ōkimi|ja|大王 (ヤマト王権)}} (Great King). Meanwhile, "造" (miyatsuko) denotes leadership over a group. Some Tomonomiyatsuko held high-ranking {{ill|Kabane|ja|カバネ}} titles like Muraji, but in a narrower sense, it referred to those with mid-to-lower ranks such as **Zō (造)** and **Obito (首, おびと)**.<ref>Kano, *Nihonshi Daijiten* (日本史大事典)</ref> Additionally, those from lower-ranking families were sometimes excluded from Tomonomiyatsuko status and categorized under the {{ill|Hyakuhachibu|ja|百八十部}} class.<ref>Hirano, *Kokushi Daijiten* (国史大辞典)</ref>
Each Tomonomiyatsuko clan had specific administrative duties and led **Be (部民)** groups, providing **human labor or material support** to the Yamato government.
Under the **Ritsuryō system**, remnants of the Tomonomiyatsuko system persisted in the **Banbu (伴部)** class, which oversaw craftsmanship and administrative work for the state. Records suggest that Banbu was still read as "Tomonomiyatsuko" in certain contexts.

'''Tomonomiyatsuko''' (伴造) were aristocratic clans of the [[Yamato Kingship]] (ヤマト王権) who managed various governmental roles, overlapping with or working under the {{ill|Muraji|ja|連}} class.
The term "伴" (tomo) is phonetically similar to "友" (companion) or "供" (attendant) and signifies service to the {{ill|Ōkimi|ja|大王 (ヤマト王権)}} (Great King). Meanwhile, "造" (miyatsuko) denotes leadership over a group. Some Tomonomiyatsuko held high-ranking {{ill|Kabane|ja|カバネ}} titles like Muraji, but in a narrower sense, it referred to those with mid-to-lower ranks such as **Zō (造)** and **Obito (首, おびと)**.<ref>Kano, *Nihonshi Daijiten* (日本史大事典)</ref> Additionally, those from lower-ranking families were sometimes excluded from Tomonomiyatsuko status and categorized under the {{ill|Hyakuhachibu|ja|百八十部}} class.<ref>Hirano, *Kokushi Daijiten* (国史大辞典)</ref>
Each Tomonomiyatsuko clan had specific administrative duties and led **Be (部民)** groups, providing **human labor or material support** to the Yamato government.
Under the **Ritsuryō system**, remnants of the Tomonomiyatsuko system persisted in the **Banbu (伴部)** class, which oversaw craftsmanship and administrative work for the state. Records suggest that Banbu was still read as "Tomonomiyatsuko" in certain contexts.

Some Tomonomiyatsuko held high-ranking {{ill|Kabane|ja|カバネ}} titles like Muraji, but in a narrower sense, it referred to those with mid-to-lower ranks such as '''Zō (造)''' and '''Obito (首, おびと)'''.<ref>Kano, *Nihonshi Daijiten* (日本史大事典)</ref> Additionally, those from lower-ranking families were sometimes excluded from Tomonomiyatsuko status and categorized under the {{ill|Hyakuhachibu|ja|百八十部}} class.<ref>Hirano, *Kokushi Daijiten* (国史大辞典)</ref>
Each Tomonomiyatsuko clan had specific administrative duties and led '''Be (部民)''' groups, providing '''human labor or material support''' to the Yamato government.
Under the '''Ritsuryō system''', remnants of the Tomonomiyatsuko system persisted in the '''Banbu (伴部)''' class, which oversaw craftsmanship and administrative work for the state. Records suggest that Banbu was still read as "Tomonomiyatsuko" in certain contexts.

Some Tomonomiyatsuko held high-ranking {{ill|Kabane|ja|カバネ|12=draft}} titles like Muraji, but in a narrower sense, it referred to those with mid-to-lower ranks such as '''Zō (造)''' and '''Obito (首, おびと)'''.<ref>Kano, *Nihonshi Daijiten* (日本史大事典)</ref> Additionally, those from lower-ranking families were sometimes excluded from Tomonomiyatsuko status and categorized under the {{ill|Hyakuhachibu|ja|百八十部|12=draft}} class.<ref>Hirano, *Kokushi Daijiten* (国史大辞典)</ref>
Each Tomonomiyatsuko clan had specific administrative duties and led '''Be (部民)''' groups, providing '''human labor or material support''' to the Yamato government.
Under the '''Ritsuryō system''', remnants of the Tomonomiyatsuko system persisted in the '''Banbu (伴部)''' class, which oversaw craftsmanship and administrative work for the state. Records suggest that Banbu was still read as "Tomonomiyatsuko" in certain contexts.
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Hirano Kunio, "Tomonomiyatsuko," in *{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa era)|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|12=draft}} 10* (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) ISBN 978-4-642-00510-4 * Kano Hisashi, "Tomonomiyatsuko," in *{{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典|12=draft}} 5* (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1993) ISBN 978-4-642-00510-4 * Mori Kōshō, "Tomonomiyatsuko," in *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=draft}} 3* (Shogakukan, 2001) ISBN 978-4-09-523003-0 == See Also == * {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度|12=draft}} * {{ill|Be System|ja|部民制|12=draft}} * {{ill|Hitosei system|ja|人制|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|国造|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kabane|ja|カバネ|12=draft}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}

revision.text:
'''伴造'''（とものみやつこ）は、[[連]]（むらじ）とも重なり、また連の下で[[ヤマト王権]]の各部司を分掌した[[豪族]]である。
「伴」は友・供と同音でヤマト王権の長である[[大王 (ヤマト王権)|大王]]（おおきみ）に奉仕する意味があり、「造」は集団の長としての意味があった。伴造には連のような上位の[[カバネ|姓]]（かばね）を持つ者も含まれるが、狭義では造・[[首 (姓)|首]]（おびと）などの中位以下の姓を持つ者を指す<ref>狩野『日本史大事典』</ref>。また、下級の姓の中には伴造から除外されて[[百八十部]]の身分に属する者もいた<ref>平野『国史大辞典』</ref>。
伴造とされた豪族はそれぞれの職掌を持ち、傘下の部民を率いてヤマト王権の中枢に人的あるいは物的に奉仕した。
律令制において[[品部]]・[[雑戸]]などを率いて器具の製作などの実務を担当する下級官人である[[伴部]]にその名残が見られ、伴部を「とものみやつこ」と読む用法が存在したことが知られている。
== 脚注 == <references/> == 参考文献 == *平野邦雄「伴造」（『[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]] 10』（[[吉川弘文館]]、1989年） ISBN 978-4-642-00510-4） *狩野久「伴造」（『[[日本史大事典]] 5』（吉川弘文館、1993年） ISBN 978-4-642-00510-4） *森公章「伴造」（『[[日本歴史大事典]] 3』（[[小学館]]、2001年） ISBN 978-4-09-523003-0）
== 関連項目 == * [[氏姓制度]] * [[部民制]] * [[人制]] * [[国造]]

Under the '''Ritsuryō system''', remnants of the Tomonomiyatsuko system persisted in the '''Banbu (伴部)''' class, which oversaw craftsmanship and administrative work for the state. Records suggest that Banbu was still read as "Tomonomiyatsuko" in certain contexts.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tomomiyatsuko]][[ja:伴造]][[zh:伴造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11381545]] Wikidata name: Tomomiyatsuko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tomonomiyatsuko''' (伴造) were aristocratic clans of the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato Kingship]] (ヤマト王権) who managed various governmental roles, overlapping with or working under the [[:en:Muraji|Muraji]] class.
The term "伴" (tomo) is phonetically similar to "友" (companion) or "供" (attendant) and signifies service to the [[:en:Okimi|Ōkimi]] (Great King). Meanwhile, "造" (miyatsuko) denotes leadership over a group. Some Tomonomiyatsuko held high-Ranking [[:en:Kabane|Kabane]] titles like Muraji, but in a narrower sense, it referred to those with mid-to-lower Ranks such as '''Zō (造)''' and '''Obito (首, おびと)'''.<ref>Kano, *Nihonshi Daijiten* (日本史大事典)</ref> Additionally, those from lower-Ranking families were sometimes excluded from Tomonomiyatsuko status and categorized under the {{ill|Hyakuhachibu|ja|百八十部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakuhachibu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} class.<ref>Hirano, *Kokushi Daijiten* (国史大辞典)</ref>
Each Tomonomiyatsuko clan had specific administrative duties and led '''Be (部民)''' groups, providing '''human labor or material support''' to the Yamato government.
Under the '''Ritsuryō system''', remnants of the Tomonomiyatsuko system persisted in the '''Banbu (伴部)''' class, which oversaw craftsmanship and administrative work for the state. Records suggest that Banbu was still read as "Tomonomiyatsuko" in certain contexts.
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Hirano Kunio, "Tomonomiyatsuko," in *[[:en:Kokushi Daijiten|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa era)]] 10* (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) ISBN 978-4-642-00510-4 * Kano Hisashi, "Tomonomiyatsuko," in *{{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonshi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 5* (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1993) ISBN 978-4-642-00510-4 * Mori Kōshō, "Tomonomiyatsuko," in *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135524488}} 3* (Shogakukan, 2001) ISBN 978-4-09-523003-0 == See Also == * {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uji-Kabane system|qq=}} * [[:en:Bemin|Be System]] * {{ill|Hitosei system|ja|人制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitosei system|qq=}} * [[:en:Kuni no miyatsuko|Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * [[:en:Kabane|Kabane]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

Under the '''Ritsuryō system''', remnants of the Tomonomiyatsuko system persisted in the '''Banbu (伴部)''' class, which oversaw craftsmanship and administrative work for the state. Records suggest that Banbu was still read as "Tomonomiyatsuko" in certain contexts.

revision.comment: Add page with conflicting Wikidata QIDs
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tomomiyatsuko]][[ja:伴造]][[zh:伴造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tomomiyatsuko]][[ja:伴造]][[zh:伴造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tomomiyatsuko]][[ja:伴造]][[zh:伴造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11381545]] Wikidata name: Tomomiyatsuko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tomonomiyatsuko''' (伴造) were aristocratic clans of the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato Kingship]] (ヤマト王権) who managed various governmental roles, overlapping with or working under the [[:en:Muraji|Muraji]] class.
The term "伴" (tomo) is phonetically similar to "友" (companion) or "供" (attendant) and signifies service to the [[:en:Okimi|Ōkimi]] (Great King). Meanwhile, "造" (miyatsuko) denotes leadership over a group. Some Tomonomiyatsuko held high-Ranking [[:en:Kabane|Kabane]] titles like Muraji, but in a narrower sense, it referred to those with mid-to-lower Ranks such as '''Zō (造)''' and '''Obito (首, おびと)'''.<ref>Kano, *Nihonshi Daijiten* (日本史大事典)</ref> Additionally, those from lower-Ranking families were sometimes excluded from Tomonomiyatsuko status and categorized under the {{ill|Hyakuhachibu|ja|百八十部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakuhachibu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} class.<ref>Hirano, *Kokushi Daijiten* (国史大辞典)</ref>
Each Tomonomiyatsuko clan had specific administrative duties and led '''Be (部民)''' groups, providing '''human labor or material support''' to the Yamato government.
Under the '''Ritsuryō system''', remnants of the Tomonomiyatsuko system persisted in the '''Banbu (伴部)''' class, which oversaw craftsmanship and administrative work for the state. Records suggest that Banbu was still read as "Tomonomiyatsuko" in certain contexts.
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Hirano Kunio, "Tomonomiyatsuko," in *[[:en:Kokushi Daijiten|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa era)]] 10* (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) ISBN 978-4-642-00510-4 * Kano Hisashi, "Tomonomiyatsuko," in *{{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonshi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 5* (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1993) ISBN 978-4-642-00510-4 * Mori Kōshō, "Tomonomiyatsuko," in *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135524488}} 3* (Shogakukan, 2001) ISBN 978-4-09-523003-0 == See Also == * {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uji-Kabane system|qq=}} * [[:en:Bemin|Be System]] * {{ill|Hitosei system|ja|人制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitosei system|qq=}} * [[:en:Kuni no miyatsuko|Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * [[:en:Kabane|Kabane]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tomomiyatsuko]][[ja:伴造]][[zh:伴造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tomomiyatsuko]][[ja:伴造]][[zh:伴造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tomomiyatsuko]][[ja:伴造]][[zh:伴造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11381545]] Wikidata name: Tomomiyatsuko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tomonomiyatsuko''' (伴造) were aristocratic clans of the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato Kingship]] (ヤマト王権) who managed various governmental roles, overlapping with or working under the [[:en:Muraji|Muraji]] class.
The term "伴" (tomo) is phonetically similar to "友" (companion) or "供" (attendant) and signifies service to the [[:en:Okimi|Ōkimi]] (Great King). Meanwhile, "造" (miyatsuko) denotes leadership over a group. Some Tomonomiyatsuko held high-Ranking [[:en:Kabane|Kabane]] titles like Muraji, but in a narrower sense, it referred to those with mid-to-lower Ranks such as '''Zō (造)''' and '''Obito (首, おびと)'''.<ref>Kano, *Nihonshi Daijiten* (日本史大事典)</ref> Additionally, those from lower-Ranking families were sometimes excluded from Tomonomiyatsuko status and categorized under the {{ill|Hyakuhachibu|ja|百八十部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakuhachibu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} class.<ref>Hirano, *Kokushi Daijiten* (国史大辞典)</ref>
Each Tomonomiyatsuko clan had specific administrative duties and led '''Be (部民)''' groups, providing '''human labor or material support''' to the Yamato government.
Under the '''Ritsuryō system''', remnants of the Tomonomiyatsuko system persisted in the '''Banbu (伴部)''' class, which oversaw craftsmanship and administrative work for the state. Records suggest that Banbu was still read as "Tomonomiyatsuko" in certain contexts.
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Hirano Kunio, "Tomonomiyatsuko," in *[[:en:Kokushi Daijiten|Kokushi Daijiten (Showa era)]] 10* (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) ISBN 978-4-642-00510-4 * Kano Hisashi, "Tomonomiyatsuko," in *{{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonshi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 5* (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1993) ISBN 978-4-642-00510-4 * Mori Kōshō, "Tomonomiyatsuko," in *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135524488}} 3* (Shogakukan, 2001) ISBN 978-4-09-523003-0 == See Also == * {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uji-Kabane system|qq=}} * [[:en:Bemin|Be System]] * {{ill|Hitosei system|ja|人制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitosei system|qq=}} * [[:en:Kuni no miyatsuko|Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * [[:en:Kabane|Kabane]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|伴造|version=101000322|comment=Imported full ja history}}

'''Tomonomiyatsuko''' (伴造) were aristocratic clans of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Kingship|1=Yamato Kingship}} (ヤマト王権) who managed various governmental roles, overlapping with or working under the {{ill|Muraji|en|Muraji|WD=Q2735611|lt=Muraji|1=Muraji}} class.
The term "伴" (tomo) is phonetically similar to "友" (companion) or "供" (attendant) and signifies service to the {{ill|Okimi|en|Okimi|WD=Q718995|lt=Ōkimi|1=Okimi}} (Great King). Meanwhile, "造" (miyatsuko) denotes leadership over a group. Some Tomonomiyatsuko held high-Ranking {{ill|Kabane|en|Kabane|WD=Q1664099|lt=Kabane|1=Kabane}} titles like Muraji, but in a narrower sense, it referred to those with mid-to-lower Ranks such as '''Zō (造)''' and '''Obito (首, おびと)'''.<ref>Kano, *Nihonshi Daijiten* (日本史大事典)</ref> Additionally, those from lower-Ranking families were sometimes excluded from Tomonomiyatsuko status and categorized under the {{ill|Hyakuhachibu|ja|百八十部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyakuhachibu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} class.<ref>Hirano, *Kokushi Daijiten* (国史大辞典)</ref>
Each Tomonomiyatsuko clan had specific administrative duties and led '''Be (部民)''' groups, providing '''human labor or material support''' to the Yamato government.
Under the '''Ritsuryō system''', remnants of the Tomonomiyatsuko system persisted in the '''Banbu (伴部)''' class, which oversaw craftsmanship and administrative work for the state. Records suggest that Banbu was still read as "Tomonomiyatsuko" in certain contexts.
== References == <references/>
== Bibliography == * Hirano Kunio, "Tomonomiyatsuko," in *{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|en|Kokushi Daijiten|WD=Q11420603|lt=Kokushi Daijiten (Showa era)|1=Kokushi Daijiten}} 10* (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1989) ISBN 978-4-642-00510-4 * Kano Hisashi, "Tomonomiyatsuko," in *{{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonshi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 5* (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1993) ISBN 978-4-642-00510-4 * Mori Kōshō, "Tomonomiyatsuko," in *{{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135524488|1=Nihon Rekishi Daijiten}} 3* (Shogakukan, 2001) ISBN 978-4-09-523003-0 == See Also == * {{ill|Uji-Kabane system|ja|氏姓制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uji-Kabane system|qq=}} * {{ill|Bemin|en|Bemin|WD=Q2892644|lt=Be System|1=Bemin}} * {{ill|Hitosei system|ja|人制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitosei system|qq=}} * {{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko|en|Kuni no miyatsuko|WD=Q2483673|lt=Kuni no Miyatsuko|1=Kuni no miyatsuko}} * {{ill|Kabane|en|Kabane|WD=Q1664099|lt=Kabane|1=Kabane}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}
